claims.claim: Within the 800–1200 K temperature range, ignition delay time decreases by approximately one order of magnitude for every 100 K increase in temperature for lithium-ion battery TR gases.
claims.quote: A strong inverse correlation exists between temperature and ignition delay time across the 800–1200 K range, with delay times decreasing by approximately one order of magnitude per 100 K temperature increase.

claims.claim: Above 1200 K, the temperature dependence of ignition delay time shows marked attenuation, with significantly reduced sensitivity compared to the lower temperature regime.
claims.quote: Above 1200 K, this temperature dependence shows marked attenuation, revealing significantly reduced sensitivity compared to the lower temperature regime.

claims.claim: Under identical equivalence ratio conditions, the laminar burning velocity of TR gases follows the descending order: LCO/NMC > NMC > LFP.
claims.quote: Under identical equivalence ratio conditions, the LBV of TR gases follows the descending order: LCO/NMC > NMC > LFP.

claims.claim: The laminar burning velocity (LBV) and laminar flame temperature of TR gases from lithium-ion batteries first increase and then decrease as the equivalence ratio transitions from lean to rich fuel conditions.
claims.quote: All three lithium-ion battery TR gases exhibit the trend of an initial increase followed by subsequent decrease in both LBV and flame temperature when transitioning from lean fuel to rich fuel conditions.

claims.claim: The adiabatic overpressure of lithium-ion battery TR gases exhibits greater sensitivity to equivalence ratio under lean fuel conditions than under rich fuel conditions.
claims.quote: The equilibrium pressure of lithium-ion battery TR gases exhibits greater sensitivity to equivalence ratio under lean fuel conditions compared to rich fuel conditions, demonstrating a characteristic trend of rapid escalation followed by gradual attenuation with increasing equivalence ratio.

claims.claim: LFP batteries exhibit higher explosion propensity than NCA batteries, primarily due to higher concentrations of ethylene and ethane in their vent gases.
claims.quote: lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries exhibit a higher explosion propensity than Nickel Cobalt Aluminum (NCA) batteries, primarily attributed to higher concentrations of ethylene and ethane.

claims.claim: The explosion tendency of LFP battery vent gases increases nonlinearly with SOC, peaking at SOC between 50% and 100%, and is higher at these SOCs than at overcharged states.
claims.quote: The explosion tendency of LFP BVGs increases nonlinearly with SOC, peaking at SOC between 50 % and 100 %.

claims.claim: Among individual BVG components under stoichiometric conditions, ethylene (C2H4) exhibits the highest explosion propensity and carbon monoxide (CO) exhibits the lowest.
claims.quote: under stoichiometric conditions, CO exhibits the lowest explosion propensity, while C2H4 demonstrates the strongest.

claims.claim: The largest difference in explosion-initiating temperatures between individual BVG components is between C2H4 and CO, with a temperature gap of up to 300 K under the same pressure conditions.
claims.quote: The largest difference in explosion-initiating temperatures is observed between C2H4 and CO, with a temperature gap of up to 300 K under the same pressure conditions.

claims.claim: The explosion limits of mixed BVG gas mixtures cannot be approximated by weighted summation of the explosion limits of individual BVG components.
claims.quote: the explosion limits of gas mixtures consisting of multiple components cannot be mathematically approximated through weighted summation of individual BVG explosion limits.

claims.claim: The ignition energy of BVG decreases linearly with increasing initial temperature.
claims.quote: The results indicate that the ignition energy of BVG decreases linearly with increasing initial temperature

claims.claim: At an equivalence ratio of 1, the minimum ignition energies of BVG at initial temperatures of 30°C, 60°C, 90°C, and 120°C are 0.12 mJ, 0.1 mJ, 0.08 mJ, and 0.063 mJ, respectively.
claims.quote: at an equivalence ratio of 1, the ignition energies at the initial temperatures of 30°C, 60°C, 90°C, and 120°C were recorded as 0.12 mJ, 0.1 mJ, 0.08 mJ, and 0.063 mJ, respectively

claims.claim: The ignition energy of BVG/DMC mixture exhibits a U-shaped trend with changes in equivalence ratio.
claims.quote: the ignition energy of the BVG-DMC mixture exhibits a U-shaped trend with changes in equivalence ratio

claims.claim: At equivalence ratio of 1.3, DMC reaches its minimum ignition energy of 0.84 mJ, which is lower than BVG's ignition energy at the same equivalence ratio.
claims.quote: the ignition energy of DMC is strongly influenced by the equivalence ratio...reaches a minimum of 0.84 mJ at the equivalence ratio of 1.3, and the ignition energy is 0.99 mJ at the equivalence ratio of 1.5, which is smaller than that of BVG at the same equivalence

claims.claim: Under lean combustion (equivalence ratio of 0.7), ignition energy of BVG/DMC mixture increases significantly with rising DMC proportion, from 0.125 mJ at 10% DMC to 8.1 mJ at 50% DMC, where 50% DMC exceeds the experimental measurement range.
claims.quote: Under the equivalence ratio of 0.7 (lean combustion), the ignition energy increases significantly with the rise in DMC proportion. The ignition energy rises from 0.125 mJ at φDMC = 10 % to 2.83 mJ at φDMC = 30 %. When φDMC reaches 50 %, the ignition energy reaches 8.1 mJ, which exceeds the experimental range
